AUT.name: Austria
AUT.note: EU member state; EU Taxonomy Regulation (EU 2020/852) applies directly.
features: Defines which economic activities count as environmentally sustainable, under the EU Taxonomy Regulation (EU 2020/852).

An activity must substantially contribute to at least one of six environmental objectives: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, sustainable use/protection of water and marine resources, transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and protection/restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems.
Must comply with the "Do No Significant Harm" (DNSH) principle toward the other five objectives.
Must meet Minimum Social Safeguards (human rights, labour rights, responsible business conduct).
Must meet Technical Screening Criteria (TSC) set by the European Commission.
Distinguishes "taxonomy-eligible" activities (covered by the taxonomy) from "taxonomy-aligned" activities (which meet all criteria).
Large companies covered by the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) must disclose their taxonomy eligibility and alignment.
Not a mandatory investment list, not a "greenness" rating of companies, and not a judgement of financial performance — activities excluded from the list are not automatically "polluting".

facts.mandatory: Mandatory disclosure for large companies under the CSRD

fullDescription: The EU Taxonomy Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2020/852) entered into force on 12 July 2020 and applies directly in all EU member states, including Austria — it does not need to be separately transposed into national law the way a directive would. It sets out four overarching conditions an economic activity must meet to qualify as environmentally sustainable: it must make a substantial contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, do no significant harm (DNSH) to the other five, comply with minimum social safeguards (covering human rights, labour rights and business conduct), and meet technical screening criteria (TSC) set out in delegated acts.

The six environmental objectives are climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, the sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources, the transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and the protection and restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems. The first two objectives were covered by the Climate Delegated Act (in force from January 2022), later joined by a Complementary Climate Delegated Act (from January 2023) that added strict, conditional criteria for specific nuclear and natural gas activities as transitional bridge technologies. The remaining four objectives were added by the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024).

Large companies operating in Austria, as elsewhere in the EU, must disclose under Article 8 of the Regulation what share of their turnover, capital expenditure and operating expenditure is 'taxonomy-eligible' (covered by the taxonomy at all) versus 'taxonomy-aligned' (actually meeting all four conditions) — this sits alongside broader sustainability reporting obligations under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). The Commission has been actively simplifying the framework: a Delegated Act reducing reporting burden was adopted in 2025 as part of the 'Omnibus' simplification package, and in March 2026 the Commission proposed further revisions to the Climate and Environmental Delegated Acts' technical screening criteria, with the revised criteria expected to apply from January 2027.

activityList.criteria: Electricity, heat and gas generation must meet a life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions threshold under the Climate Delegated Act; solar and wind generation are automatically recognised, while specific nuclear and natural gas activities can qualify only under strict conditions introduced by the Complementary Climate Delegated Act (e.g. permitting deadlines and replacement of higher-emitting capacity).
activityList.threshold: Emissions thresholds set out in Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2139, as revised
activityList.dnsh: Applies to water, circular economy, pollution and biodiversity objectives

activityList.criteria: New construction must qualify as a Nearly Zero-Energy Building (NZEB) with primary energy demand below the applicable national NZEB threshold; major renovations must meet national building-renovation requirements or deliver substantial energy savings; individual renovation measures (insulation, efficient heating, renewables) can also qualify.
activityList.threshold: NZEB and renovation thresholds under the Climate Delegated Act
activityList.dnsh: Applies, particularly to water and circular economy objectives

activityList.criteria: Covers low- and zero-emission vehicles, rail transport, and supporting infrastructure (e.g. charging points); passenger car criteria tighten over time towards a zero direct-emissions requirement.
activityList.threshold: Vehicle and infrastructure criteria under the Climate Delegated Act

activityList.criteria: Covers manufacture of low-carbon technologies and components (e.g. batteries, hydrogen equipment, energy-efficiency equipment) as well as emissions-intensive processes (cement, steel, chemicals, aluminium) assessed against sector-specific benchmarks.
activityList.threshold: Sector benchmarks under the Climate Delegated Act, often referencing EU ETS best-performer benchmarks

activityList.criteria: Covers water collection, treatment and supply infrastructure, and wastewater treatment, assessed for water-efficiency and leakage performance and contribution to the water and marine resources objective.
activityList.threshold: Criteria under the Environmental Delegated Act
activityList.dnsh: Applies, particularly to pollution and biodiversity objectives

activityList.criteria: Covers farming practices and land use that substantially contribute to biodiversity protection, circular economy or pollution-prevention objectives, added later than the core climate criteria.
activityList.threshold: Criteria under the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024)

activityList.criteria: Covers data centres (assessed against energy- and resource-efficiency benchmarks broadly aligned with the EU Code of Conduct for Data Centre Energy Efficiency) and digital solutions that enable measurable GHG reductions in other sectors.
activityList.threshold: Efficiency benchmarks under the Climate Delegated Act

It sets out four overarching conditions an economic activity must meet to qualify as environmentally sustainable: it must make a substantial contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, do no significant harm (DNSH) to the other five, comply with minimum social safeguards (covering human rights, labour rights and business conduct), and meet technical screening criteria (TSC) set out in delegated acts.

The six environmental objectives are climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, the sustainable use and protection of water and marine resources, the transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and the protection and restoration of biodiversity and ecosystems. The first two objectives were covered by the Climate Delegated Act (in force from January 2022), later joined by a Complementary Climate Delegated Act (from January 2023) that added strict, conditional criteria for specific nuclear and natural gas activities as transitional bridge technologies. The remaining four objectives were added by the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024).

Large companies operating in Greece, as elsewhere in the EU, must disclose under Article 8 of the Regulation what share of their turnover, capital expenditure and operating expenditure is 'taxonomy-eligible' (covered by the taxonomy at all) versus 'taxonomy-aligned' (actually meeting all four conditions) — this sits alongside broader sustainability reporting obligations under the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD). The Commission has been actively simplifying the framework: a Delegated Act reducing reporting burden was adopted in 2025 as part of the 'Omnibus' simplification package, and in March 2026 the Commission proposed further revisions to the Climate and Environmental Delegated Acts' technical screening criteria, with the revised criteria expected to apply from January 2027.

activityList.criteria: Electricity, heat and gas generation must meet a life-cycle greenhouse gas emissions threshold under the Climate Delegated Act; solar and wind generation are automatically recognised, while specific nuclear and natural gas activities can qualify only under strict conditions introduced by the Complementary Climate Delegated Act (e.g. permitting deadlines and replacement of higher-emitting capacity).
activityList.threshold: Emissions thresholds set out in Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2139, as revised
activityList.dnsh: Applies to water, circular economy, pollution and biodiversity objectives

activityList.criteria: New construction must qualify as a Nearly Zero-Energy Building (NZEB) with primary energy demand below the applicable national NZEB threshold; major renovations must meet national building-renovation requirements or deliver substantial energy savings; individual renovation measures (insulation, efficient heating, renewables) can also qualify.
activityList.threshold: NZEB and renovation thresholds under the Climate Delegated Act
activityList.dnsh: Applies, particularly to water and circular economy objectives

activityList.criteria: Covers low- and zero-emission vehicles, rail transport, and supporting infrastructure (e.g. charging points); passenger car criteria tighten over time towards a zero direct-emissions requirement.
activityList.threshold: Vehicle and infrastructure criteria under the Climate Delegated Act

activityList.criteria: Covers manufacture of low-carbon technologies and components (e.g. batteries, hydrogen equipment, energy-efficiency equipment) as well as emissions-intensive processes (cement, steel, chemicals, aluminium) assessed against sector-specific benchmarks.
activityList.threshold: Sector benchmarks under the Climate Delegated Act, often referencing EU ETS best-performer benchmarks

activityList.criteria: Covers water collection, treatment and supply infrastructure, and wastewater treatment, assessed for water-efficiency and leakage performance and contribution to the water and marine resources objective.
activityList.threshold: Criteria under the Environmental Delegated Act
activityList.dnsh: Applies, particularly to pollution and biodiversity objectives

activityList.criteria: Covers farming practices and land use that substantially contribute to biodiversity protection, circular economy or pollution-prevention objectives, added later than the core climate criteria.
activityList.threshold: Criteria under the Environmental Delegated Act (in force from January 2024)

activityList.criteria: Covers data centres (assessed against energy- and resource-efficiency benchmarks broadly aligned with the EU Code of Conduct for Data Centre Energy Efficiency) and digital solutions that enable measurable GHG reductions in other sectors.
activityList.threshold: Efficiency benchmarks under the Climate Delegated Act

KOR.name: South Korea
KOR.note: Established by the Ministry of Climate, Energy and Environment (MCEE) in late 2021. Based on Substantial Contribution, DNSH and Minimum Safeguards; 100 activities (93 Green area, 7 Transitional area).
features: Established by the Ministry of Climate, Energy and Environment (MCEE) in late 2021 to support South Korea's 2050 carbon-neutrality goal.
Based on three principles: Substantial Contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, Do No Significant Harm (DNSH), and Minimum Safeguards (MS).
Six environmental objectives: greenhouse gas reduction, climate change adaptation, sustainable water management, circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and biodiversity conservation.

Two categories covering 100 activities total: a Green area (93 activities that directly contribute to carbon neutrality) and a Transitional area (7 activities, e.g. nuclear power and LNG-related activities, considered necessary bridges toward a low-carbon economy).

Alignment requires passing a four-step Alignment Assessment Process: Activity Criteria → Recognition Criteria (technical thresholds) → Exclusion Criteria (DNSH) → Protection Criteria (Minimum Safeguards).
Widely used for green bonds, green loans, green asset-backed securities, sustainable investment funds, and ESG disclosures.
KOR.regulator: Ministry of Climate, Energy and Environment (MCEE)

facts.euAlignment: Closely modelled on the EU Taxonomy, with added transition activities
facts.mandatory: Used for green bond/loan verification; not a general mandatory disclosure regime

fullDescription: South Korea's K-Taxonomy (Korean Green Taxonomy) was established by the (then) Ministry of Environment in December 2021, under the Environmental Technology and Environmental Industry Support Act, as part of the country's push toward its 2050 carbon-neutrality goal. It is built on the same three core principles used by most major taxonomies: an activity must make a Substantial Contribution to at least one of six environmental objectives, satisfy Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) toward the others, and meet Minimum Safeguards on social and governance conduct.

The six environmental objectives are greenhouse gas reduction, climate change adaptation, sustainable water management, transition to a circular economy, pollution prevention and control, and biodiversity conservation. Activities are organised into two areas: a Green area (currently 93 activities that directly and substantially contribute to carbon neutrality, spanning energy, transport, manufacturing, water and buildings) and a smaller Transitional area (7 activities, including specific nuclear power and LNG-related activities, treated as bridge technologies toward a low-carbon economy rather than end-state green activities).

Alignment is assessed through a four-step process: Activity Criteria (is the activity on the list at all), Recognition Criteria (does it meet the technical/quantitative thresholds), Exclusion Criteria (does it avoid significant harm to the other objectives), and Protection Criteria (does it meet minimum safeguards). In December 2024, the guidelines were updated to add 10 new eligible activities and revise 21 existing ones — particularly around water conservation, the circular economy, pollution control and biodiversity — introducing 25 new and 6 updated technical criteria. The K-Taxonomy is used mainly for green bond and green loan verification, and increasingly as the reference point for banks' green finance guidelines, rather than as an economy-wide mandatory disclosure regime.

activityList.criteria: Renewable power generation (solar, wind, hydro, geothermal) and energy-efficiency/storage infrastructure that substantially reduces greenhouse gas emissions.

activityList.criteria: Low- and zero-emission vehicles, railways, and supporting infrastructure such as EV charging networks.
activityList.threshold: Sector-specific recognition criteria under the K-Taxonomy Guideline

activityList.criteria: Manufacture of low-carbon and energy-efficient products, and process improvements that measurably cut emissions intensity.
activityList.threshold: Sector-specific recognition criteria under the K-Taxonomy Guideline

fullDescription: Brunei is one of the ten ASEAN member states covered by the ASEAN Taxonomy for Sustainable Finance, a regional framework developed by the ASEAN Taxonomy Board rather than a purely national one. It is explicitly built around the principle of a 'just and orderly transition' — recognising that member states are at very different stages of economic development and allowing each country flexibility in how it applies the framework, rather than imposing a single uniform pathway.

The taxonomy defines four environmental objectives: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, protection of healthy ecosystems and biodiversity, and resource resilience/transition to a circular economy. It uses a distinctive traffic-light classification instead of a simple pass/fail test: activities are graded Green (substantial contribution), Amber (a credible transition pathway, not yet fully aligned) or Red (not aligned, or causing significant harm).

Two assessment approaches sit underneath this: the Foundation Framework, a qualitative, principles-based checklist intended to be accessible to a wide range of users, and the more rigorous Plus Standard, which applies quantitative technical screening criteria split into Tier 1 Green, Tier 2 Amber and Tier 3 Amber. Distinctively, the framework allows 'remedial measures to transition' — an activity causing some harm can still qualify if a credible plan exists to eliminate that harm within five years. The ASEAN Taxonomy was designed with interoperability with the EU Taxonomy in mind, though as a regional reference framework it is voluntary rather than a mandatory national disclosure regime.

features: Combines environmental, social and economic sustainability objectives by integrating the UN Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) alongside environmental criteria.

Covers seven focus areas: energy conservation & efficiency, industrial decarbonisation, digitalisation & smart manufacturing, greenhouse gas control & carbon capture (CCUS), circular economy & resource efficiency, pollution prevention and control, and biodiversity/ecosystem protection.
Places strong emphasis on transforming heavy industry (steel, cement, chemicals), rather than focusing mainly on renewable energy as many Western taxonomies do.
Treats carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS) as a key recognised transition technology for large-scale emissions reduction in carbon-intensive industries.
Technology-neutral: activities can qualify through energy efficiency gains, process innovation, digitalisation or resource efficiency, not only through a single clean technology.
Designed to support green bonds, sustainable lending, green investment products and other environmental financing mechanisms.

facts.dnsh: Not formally specified — focuses on environmental outcomes
facts.euAlignment: Distinct SDG-oriented approach; partial interoperability efforts underway
facts.mandatory: Used to guide green bond and lending eligibility

fullDescription: China's SDG Finance Taxonomy takes a broader approach than most Western frameworks by combining environmental sustainability with the UN Sustainable Development Goals, rather than focusing narrowly on 'green' activities. It was published with People's Bank of China involvement and reflects China's specific industrial structure, placing particular emphasis on transforming heavy, emissions-intensive industry (steel, cement, chemicals) rather than centring mainly on renewable energy deployment.

The framework organises eligible activities into seven focus areas: energy conservation and efficiency, industrial decarbonisation, digitalisation and smart manufacturing, greenhouse gas control and carbon capture, utilisation and storage (CCUS), circular economy and resource efficiency, pollution prevention and control, and biodiversity and ecosystem protection. It treats CCUS as a strategically important transition technology for large-scale emissions reduction in carbon-intensive industry — a more prominent role than in most other taxonomies.

The taxonomy is technology-neutral: activities can qualify through energy-efficiency gains, process innovation, digitalisation or resource efficiency, not only via a single 'clean technology' pathway. It is primarily used to guide green bond and green lending eligibility. Formal DNSH and minimum-safeguards requirements are less codified than in the EU or Korean frameworks, and interoperability work with other major taxonomies (including the EU's) is ongoing but partial.

features: A voluntary classification system (not a mandatory regulatory requirement).

Based on four elements: Technical Screening Criteria (TSC), Do No Significant Harm (DNSH), Minimum Social Safeguards (MSS), and science-based emissions thresholds.

facts.mandatory: Voluntary

fullDescription: The Australian Sustainable Finance Taxonomy is a voluntary classification system developed by the Australian Sustainable Finance Institute (ASFI), rather than a government-mandated regulatory requirement. It currently focuses on climate change mitigation, with particular emphasis on transition finance for emissions-intensive sectors of the Australian economy (such as mining, agriculture and heavy industry) that need credible pathways to decarbonise rather than being excluded outright.

It is built on four elements: Technical Screening Criteria (TSC) for eligible activities, Do No Significant Harm (DNSH) safeguards, Minimum Social Safeguards, and science-based emissions thresholds aligned with a 1.5°C pathway. Activities are classified as either 'Green' (already aligned with a 1.5°C pathway) or 'Transition' (contributing meaningfully to decarbonisation but not yet fully aligned) — a two-tier structure designed to bring emissions-intensive but transitioning activities into the scope of sustainable finance rather than leaving them entirely outside it.

Because it was developed independently of the EU framework, the Australian taxonomy is not legally interoperable with the EU Taxonomy, but it was designed with broadly similar principles (substantial contribution, DNSH, safeguards) to support cross-border comparability for investors. It is intended to support sustainable investment product labelling, green and transition finance, corporate disclosures, and credible transition planning across the Australian market.

SEN.name: Senegal
SEN.note: Under development by the Ministry of Environment and Ecological Transition and the Ministry of Finance and Budget, with technical and financial support from German development cooperation (GIZ, via the 2025–2028 'Finance Durable' project). First phase launched March 2024, second phase October 2024, with a restitution/validation workshop on the first draft held in September 2025. A February 2025 news report described Senegal as having 'adopted' the taxonomy, but GIZ's own February 2025 project factsheet describes the national taxonomy as still 'en cours d'élaboration' (in development) — not yet finalised as of the most recent public information found. Would be the first in francophone Africa once complete, aiming for interoperability with the EU and Rwandan taxonomies.

officialDocuments.title: GIZ — Renforcer la finance durable au Sénégal (« Finance Durable ») project factsheet

CAN.name: Canada
CAN.note: The Sustainable Finance Action Council (SFAC), whose mandate concluded in March 2024, endorsed a Taxonomy Roadmap Report in 2022 with 10 recommendations. In December 2025 the federal government announced two years of seed funding for a new governing council to finalise voluntary 'Made-in-Canada' sustainable investment guidelines, targeting three priority sectors (electricity, transportation, buildings, agriculture/forestry, manufacturing, extractives) by end of 2026 and further sectors by fall 2027. Not yet finalised.

officialDocuments.title: Government of Canada — Taxonomy Roadmap Report
Government of Canada — announcement of next steps toward made-in-Canada sustainable investment guidelines

CAN.regulator: Department of Finance Canada / Sustainable Finance Action Council

ALB.name: Albania
ALB.note: The Bank of Albania, with support from the European Investment Bank, is developing a national Green Taxonomy aligned with EU standards as part of its EU accession commitments (Chapter 9, Financial Services), alongside a Climate Risk Management Guide and a sustainability-monitoring Green Dashboard; not yet finalised.

officialDocuments.title: Bank of Albania — green strategy progress update (May 2023–2024)

ETH.name: Ethiopia
ETH.note: The National Bank of Ethiopia is developing a National Green Taxonomy under the Greening the Financial Systems Programme (technical agreement signed May 2025, funded by Germany and Luxembourg, supported by the European Investment Bank), to guide financial institutions and investors on environmentally sustainable economic activities. Not yet finalised.

officialDocuments.title: European Investment Bank — Ethiopia Unveils Strategic Initiative to Green Its Financial System

ETH.regulator: National Bank of Ethiopia (framework in development)

GHA.name: Ghana
GHA.note: The Ministry of Finance launched Phase 1 of the Ghana Green Finance Taxonomy on 9 October 2024 at the SDGs Investment Fair, to classify environmentally sustainable economic activities for financial institutions, investors and policymakers. A Phase 2 (quantitative thresholds, tax incentives) and Phase 3 (transitional taxonomy for carbon-intensive sectors) are planned. The Bank of Ghana separately issued a Climate-Related Financial Risk Directive in May 2024.

officialDocuments.title: Ministry of Finance — Ghana Green Finance Taxonomy (official PDF)

GHA.regulator: Ministry of Finance / Bank of Ghana

IND.name: India
IND.note: Announced in the Union Budget FY2024-25, the Ministry of Finance (Department of Economic Affairs) published a Draft Framework of India's Climate Finance Taxonomy in May 2025 for public consultation (closed 25 June 2025), drawing on the IPSF Common Ground Taxonomy and covering power, mobility, buildings, agriculture, food security, water security and hard-to-abate sectors. A sector-specific Green Steel Taxonomy was released in December 2024. Rollout is expected in phases from 2025 to 2027; the economy-wide taxonomy is not yet finalised.

officialDocuments.title: Ministry of Finance (PIB) — Draft Framework of India's Climate Finance Taxonomy
Press Information Bureau — public consultation notice on the draft taxonomy

IND.regulator: Ministry of Finance (Dept. of Economic Affairs) / SEBI

MAR.name: Morocco
MAR.note: Following Morocco's Climate Finance Strategy (Sep 2024), the Ministry of Finance, Bank Al-Maghrib, the AMMC and ACAPS are developing a Green Finance Taxonomy with World Bank support (tender issued, deadline Mar 2025), prioritising climate adaptation, mitigation, water/marine resources and biodiversity. Bank Al-Maghrib separately issued climate-disclosure directives in 2025. Not yet finalised.

officialDocuments.title: World Bank — Development of a Green Finance Taxonomy for Morocco (project documentation)

MAR.regulator: Bank Al-Maghrib / Ministry of Finance (framework in development)

NZL.name: New Zealand
NZL.taxonomy: NZ Taxonomy (Aotearoa New Zealand Sustainable Finance Taxonomy)
NZL.note: Being developed by the Centre for Sustainable Finance in partnership with the New Zealand Government. A first sector taxonomy — NZ Taxonomy for Agriculture and Forestry, Version 1 — has been published for voluntary use, with further sectors (Construction and Buildings, Energy, Industrial Manufacturing, Transport) still in progress; designed to be interoperable with the EU, Australian and ASEAN taxonomies. Not yet a complete, economy-wide framework.

officialDocuments.title: Centre for Sustainable Finance — Developing a Sustainable Finance Taxonomy for Aotearoa New Zealand (Full Recommendations Report)
Centre for Sustainable Finance — NZ Taxonomy (official page)

NZL.regulator: Reserve Bank of New Zealand / Centre for Sustainable Finance

PNG.name: Papua New Guinea
PNG.note: The Bank of Papua New Guinea officially launched the Inclusive Green Finance Taxonomy Version 2.0, alongside Standards for Green Loan Classification and Reporting and an Environmental and Social Risk Management Guideline, at the 2026 Green Finance Summit in Port Moresby (27-28 May 2026). Several banks (BSP, MiBank, Mama Bank, TISA) are piloting the taxonomy.

officialDocuments.title: Green Finance Centre PNG — Green Finance Summit 2026 summary
Sustainable Banking and Finance Network — 2026 ESRM Guideline and Taxonomy Standards Workshops and Green Finance Summit

PER.name: Peru
PER.note: The Peruvian Government created a Multisectoral Commission on Green Finance Taxonomy via Supreme Decree No. 154-2025-EF (26 Jul 2025), chaired by the Ministry of Economy and Finance with ten public entities. The commission has a 360-day mandate to diagnose the state of green finance and produce a technical taxonomy proposal, expected mid-2026. Not yet finalised.

ECU.name: Ecuador
ECU.note: The Ministry of Economy and Finance, Ministry of Environment and the National Planning Secretariat formalised a Sustainable Finance Table (Mesa de Finanzas Sostenibles) in October 2024, tasked with developing a green/social/sustainable taxonomy. In December 2025 Ecuador published its Roadmap for the Green Taxonomy (Hoja de Ruta), developed with the Global Green Growth Institute, setting out the conceptual and institutional guidelines for building the taxonomy. A full national taxonomy is not yet in force.

MWI.name: Malawi
MWI.note: The Reserve Bank of Malawi, supported by the UNEP Copenhagen Climate Centre, has completed a feasibility study and internal roadmap on greening the financial sector, and announced a dedicated Climate Change Centre as a first institutional step. A green finance taxonomy itself has not yet been published.

officialDocuments.title: UNEP Copenhagen Climate Centre — Malawi Moves Towards Green Climate Finance

MWI.regulator: Reserve Bank of Malawi (framework in development)

SWZ.name: Eswatini
SWZ.note: The Central Bank of Eswatini held a Stakeholder Validation Workshop for the Eswatini Green Finance Taxonomy in May 2026, developed by a Technical Working Group including the Ministry of Tourism and Environmental Affairs, the Ministry of Finance, and the Eswatini Bankers Association, alongside a separate 'Greening of the Central Bank of Eswatini' project. Not yet finalised.

officialDocuments.title: Bank for International Settlements — Remarks: Stakeholder Validation Workshop for the Eswatini Green Finance Taxonomy

SWZ.regulator: Central Bank of Eswatini (framework in development)

MDG.name: Madagascar
MDG.note: Madagascar participates in continental initiatives such as the African Development Bank's African Green Banks Initiative and regional work toward a Continental Sustainable Finance Taxonomy, but no dedicated national green finance taxonomy document specific to Madagascar was identified as of this review. Not yet finalised.

officialDocuments.title: African Development Bank — African Green Banks Initiative (official page)

MDG.regulator: Central Bank of Madagascar (framework in development)

TUR.name: Turkey
TUR.note: Turkey’s Climate Change Presidency (Ministry of Environment, Urbanisation and Climate Change) published a Draft Green Taxonomy Regulation in 2024, closely modelled on the EU Taxonomy. Mandatory taxonomy reporting for investment firms, funds and insurers is planned from 1 January 2027, with a voluntary transition period to 31 December 2026; the regulation is not yet finalised.
TUR.regulator: Central Bank of the Republic of Turkey

officialDocuments.title: Draft Regulation on the Türkiye Green Taxonomy — overview (Tevetoglu Legal)

ARM.name: Armenia
ARM.note: The Central Bank of Armenia published a National Sustainable Finance Roadmap in October 2023. A separate ‘Developing Green Taxonomy in Armenia’ project (World Bank Group, Ministry of Economy of Armenia, and the American University of Armenia) is building the underlying policy and legal framework; a taxonomy is not yet finalised.
ARM.regulator: Central Bank of Armenia (framework in development)

officialDocuments.title: Central Bank of Armenia — National Sustainable Finance Roadmap of Armenia (official PDF)

HND.name: Honduras
HND.note: Launched by the National Banking and Insurance Commission (CNBS) with technical assistance from IFC, establishing sustainability criteria, do-no-significant-harm (DNSH) requirements, social safeguards and verifiable indicators for renewable energy, agroindustry, efficient manufacturing and green infrastructure. Honduras is also part of a regional Central American green taxonomy presented via CCSBSO.

ARG.name: Argentina
ARG.note: The Central Bank of Argentina (BCRA) approved a National Strategy for Sustainable Finance (ENFS), developed through the Technical Table of Sustainable Finance (Mesa Técnica de Finanzas Sostenibles, est. 2020). The strategy's five pillars include a 'Common Language and Taxonomy' workstream, with initial sector focus on Agriculture/Livestock/Fishing, Energy and Mining. A finalised taxonomy is not yet in force.

URY.name: Uruguay
URY.note: A Sustainable Finance Roundtable (est. December 2021) coordinates public and private sector work on sustainable finance. A green taxonomy is in development with support from UNEP FI and funding from the Climate Technology Centre and Network (CTCN); Uruguay's Central Bank has also published a sustainability roadmap. Not yet finalised.

UKR.name: Ukraine
UKR.note: Ukraine has launched a Sustainable Finance Platform and plans to implement EU Taxonomy Regulation (2020/852) principles as part of its recovery and EU-accession strategy; a legally binding national taxonomy is not yet in force.

officialDocuments.title: EU Delegation to Ukraine — Empowering Ukraine's Resilience: Sustainable Finance Platform launch

GTM.name: Guatemala
GTM.note: Guatemala has launched two voluntary taxonomies via the Consejo Consultivo de Finanzas Sostenibles (Sustainable Finance Advisory Council) with IDB Invest and CentraRSE: a Social Finance Taxonomy (the first in Latin America, covering financial inclusion and housing for SMEs, women, youth and the silver economy) and a Voluntary Green Finance Taxonomy covering renewable energy, energy efficiency, clean transport, sustainable construction, circular economy and water management. Neither is yet a mandatory, economy-wide framework.

BIH.name: Bosnia and Herzegovina
BIH.note: The Central Bank of Bosnia and Herzegovina joined the Sustainable Banking and Finance Network in August 2024 and issued a Green Taxonomy in 2025 as part of a broader ESG strategy (2025–2027), with support from EIB Global on green financing and climate-risk supervision; not yet in binding force.
